items.title: Prioritizing Is the Hardest Thing Your Brain Does. Your Calendar Treats It Like the Easiest.
items.excerpt: Image Picture the meeting where your organization decides what actually matters. The backlog refinement, the portfolio review, the roadmap call where someone finally asks what you should build next and what you should drop. Now notice when it happens. Late morning after three other meetings, or worse, at four in the afternoon when everyone in the room has already spent the day deciding a hundred smaller things. The single most valuable act in any product organization, choosing what to do and what to refuse, gets handed to brains that have almost nothing left to do it with. David Rock has spent
items.titleJa: プロダクト優先順位付けと脳の疲弊のミスマッチ
items.summaryJa: プロダクト組織における「何をするか、何をしないか」の決定は最も重要な意思決定ですが、実際には多くの組織でそれが疲弊した状態で行われています。バックログ精査や優先度決定といった高度な判断が、既に大量の決定で疲労した脳に委ねられることで、本来必要な判断力が発揮されていない現状を指摘しています。

items.title: Just Looking
items.excerpt: In Looking Good, Testers! I made the case that testing is about looking and that delegating the looking to someone or something else involves a risk calculation: do you both understand the task the same way, is the third party likely to carry out the task in the way you requested, how easily can you check any results they give back, could they complement you in some way, how will you keep your knowledge and skills up to date if you don't do this task any more, how much does this task matter, how much do you care about any of that, ...? I didn't say anything there about selecting what to look a
items.titleJa: テスター自身が確認することの重要性
items.summaryJa: テストの本質は「確認する」ことであり、その役割を他者やツールに委譲する際には、理解の一致、実行の正確性、結果検証の容易性など複数のリスク要因を検討する必要があります。自動化やアウトソースの判断には、知識・スキルの維持や業務の重要度など多角的な視点が求められます。

items.title: Discovery Is De-Risking, Not Requirements Gathering
items.excerpt: Originally published on yuvalyeret.com. Most organizations I work with are very, very good at executing projects. Give them a defined scope and a date, and they will drive it to done. That muscle is a real strength. It is also the thing that quietly caps the return on their software investments. A project-execution mindset optimizes one question: did we ship what we said we would, on time? That is a fine question for building a bridge, where the requirements are knowable up front and the cost of changing your mind late is enormous. It is the wrong question for software, where the requirements
items.titleJa: ディスカバリーはリスク低減、要件定義ではない
items.summaryJa: ソフトウェア開発において、プロジェクト実行型マインドセットは「予定通り納品できたか」に最適化されるが、これは要件が事前に確定できるブリッジ建設と異なり、ソフトウェアには不適切。真のリスク低減にはディスカバリープロセスが必須。

In this Brain Sensei review, I’ll share who Brain Sensei training is most appropriate for, what to expect, the cost, the pros and cons and all the features you get included. items.title: 15 Cheap and free project communication ideas that actually work
items.excerpt: How do you manage to communicate about your project with virtually no marketing budget? This was a question someone asked me after my presentation on how to market your project at a PMI PMXPO event. We don’t all have big budgets to produce banners, T-shirts and mugs with logos on to share the messages about our projects. You do? Oh, good for you. Go and read this about the collaboration software you can use on your projects. If you are still here, then you don’t have a big budget for project communications. You are in the majority. So let’s look at what options you have for cheap or free proje
items.titleJa: 予算ゼロで実現する15のプロジェクト・コミュニケーション施策
items.summaryJa: マーケティング予算が限定的なプロジェクトマネージャー向けに、ロゴ入りグッズなど無しで低コストまたは無料で実施できるコミュニケーション施策を15紹介。多くのプロジェクト環境で実現可能な現実的なアプローチ。

items.title: EP106 AI as a Powerful Teammate in Product Management
items.excerpt: Summary In this episode, Jay Stansell interviews Dave West from Scrum.org about the evolving role of AI in product management. They explore how AI acts as a teammate, its limitations, and its impact
items.titleJa: プロダクトマネジメントにおけるAIの強力なチームメンバーシップ
items.summaryJa: Jay StansellがScrum.orgのDave Westにインタビュー。プロダクト管理業務におけるAIの役割進化、制約、および組織への影響を探索します。AIをチームメンバーとして活用する方法論を解説。

items.title: You helped your teams into helplessness
items.excerpt: The most dependent teams I've seen were built by the most generous people in the building. That isn't a coincidence. It's a pattern, and it's almost always invisible to the people causing it. Picture the support an organization builds with the best of intentions. Every team gets an agile coach with a playbook. A transformation office owns the roadmap so teams "can focus on delivery." There's a center of excellence to set the standards, a templates library, a guardrail for every decision that used to feel risky. Impediments get cleared before most teams even notice them. From the outside it loo
items.titleJa: 親切心がチームの自立性を奪う——支援の罠
items.summaryJa: 良かれと思った組織的支援（アジャイルコーチ、ロードマップ管理、CoE等）が、かえってチームを依存させてしまう構造的問題を指摘。過度な支援がチームのオーナーシップを損なう実態について解説。

items.title: Project management training to achieve certification this year
items.excerpt: The skills gap for project managers is getting worse, according to research by APM for National Apprenticeship Week. Their survey showed that 1 in 8 project managers think that the gap between what employers want and the ability of employees to meet those expectations. The survey also found that 1 in 7 project managers believe there aren't enough skilled professionals in their industry. Skilled professionals are in demand, especially those who can demonstrate adaptability, flexibility, communication, time management, risk management and leadership, according to the survey. Certification can he
items.titleJa: 今年のプロジェクトマネジメント認定資格取得に向けた研修
items.summaryJa: APMの調査によると、プロジェクトマネージャーのスキルギャップが深刻化しており、8人に1人が企業の期待と従業員の能力のギャップを認識しています。業界には適応力、柔軟性、コミュニケーション、時間管理、リスク管理、リーダーシップといったスキルを持つ専門家が不足しており、認定資格の取得はこうしたスキル習得に有効とされています。

items.title: From Chaos to Control, Part 5 - Goal
items.excerpt: Recap In the last post we saw how implementing a strict, rigorous definition of "Done" is a driver for change. Putting a lens over quality encourages team members to scrutinize their way-of-working -- perhaps one that is quite chaotic or reactive -- and to build structure and stability into it. By critically evaluating team operations, we've been able to focus on continuous quality improvement, such as by resolving workflow bottlenecks. A key discovery was that team members do not need to be cross-trained in every skill. Training employees to handle just one task to the "left or right" of thei
items.titleJa: 混乱から統制へ（その5）——ゴール設定
items.summaryJa: 「完成」の定義を厳密に設定することで、チームの品質意識が向上し、ワークフローが改善される。継続的品質向上を通じて、組織全体の構造と安定性を構築するための施策を紹介。

items.title: From Personal Productivity To AI Operating-Model Change — A Conversation With Zoetis CTO Kumar Venugopal
items.excerpt: Originally published on yuvalyeret.com. How to Scale AI from Personal Productivity to Operating Model Change Most AI efforts I see are still living inside personal productivity. People are 20% faster with ChatGPT, Claude, or Copilot, and leaders are pointing at that productivity as evidence that the AI strategy is working. It is a fine starting point, but it is not where the real value lives. The deeper opportunity is to move past individual augmentation into workflow-level and operating-model-level change — redesigning how decisions get made, how work flows, what teams own, and what the organ
items.titleJa: 個人の生産性からAI導入による経営モデル変革へ
items.summaryJa: AI活用は現在、個人の生産性向上（ChatGPTで20%高速化）に留まることが多いが、真の価値は意思決定やワークフロー、チーム体制までを変革する経営モデルレベルでの活用にある。Zoetis CTOへのインタビューで、AIスケーリングの本質に迫る。

items.title: Great Products, Bad Companies
items.excerpt: I have long argued that great products are what powers great companies. And while I still believe that great products are necessary for great companies, it’s become quite clear over the years that great products are not sufficient. In fact, as counter-intuitive as this sounds, I have seen too many examples where great products can... The post Great Products, Bad Companies appeared first on Silicon Valley Product Group.
